title: ACSI — Airfield Compliance and Safety Inspection
summary: Covers the annual DAFMAN 13-204v2 Para 5.4.3 ACSI workflow: draft creation, item pass/fail/NA responses, linked discrepancies, risk certification, and PDF filing.
concept:
The Airfield Compliance and Safety Inspection (ACSI) is the annual comprehensive safety audit mandated by DAFMAN 13-204 Volume 2, Para 5.4.3. Glidepath replaces the paper checklist with a structured digital form that persists draft state across devices, links inspection failures directly to discrepancies, and produces a signed PDF record.

Inspection lifecycle

  • Draft — a new or resumed inspection. Auto-saved to the database the moment a new ACSI is started so photo uploads have a valid acsi_inspection_id immediately.
  • In Progress — the same row transitioned back from a completed state via Reopen. The reopenAcsiInspection function rebuilds draft_data from the filed items so the form can load all prior responses.
  • Completed / Staffed — finalized by Complete & File. draft_data is set to null and the row becomes read-only except to admins.

Checklist structure

The master checklist lives in ACSI_CHECKLIST_SECTIONS in lib/constants.ts. Each section contains items with an id, a question text, an optional subsection label, an isHeading flag for non-answerable rows, and a hasSubFields flag for items that split into A/B/C sub-items. Section 10 also supports user-added local items for installation-specific concerns.

Item responses

Each answerable item accepts three states — pass (Y), fail (N), or N/A — stored in draft.responses keyed by item ID. Clicking the active button again clears the response back to null. When a response is fail, the <AcsiDiscrepancyPanelGroup> appears inline to capture discrepancy details, photos, and optionally link to an existing discrepancy record.

Risk Management Certification

At the bottom of the form, <AcsiRiskCert> collects one or more reviewer signature blocks (role/organization/rank/name/title). The certification statement — that the inspector has reviewed the results and determined deficiencies have acceptable risk control measures — is hardcoded in the component as the authoritative regulatory language.

Draft persistence pattern

Draft state uses a two-tier model: localStorage provides instant resume; the database (acsi_inspections.draft_data JSONB) provides cross-device sync. On mount the form loads from localStorage first (zero latency), then checks the DB for a newer draft. The acsiDraftToItems() helper in lib/acsi-draft.ts converts the keyed responses map into the AcsiItem[] array required by the database row's items column.

Permission gates

ACSI_WRITECan create/edit inspections and reopen completed ones
ACSI_DELETECan delete any inspection (Airfield Manager / base admin)

code.caption: ACSI list page — status filter, KPI badges, and reopen/delete permission logic
code.note: The `showReopen` and `showEdit` guards combine the permission key with an ownership check (filed_by_id / inspector_id), so the original inspector can edit their own draft without needing the full ACSI_WRITE permission.

code.caption: Data layer — saveAcsiDraft, fileAcsiInspection, and reopenAcsiInspection
code.from: /** File (finalize) an ACSI inspection: set status to completed, clear draft_data */
code.note: fileAcsiInspection sets draft_data to null and clears the saved_by/saved_at columns. reopenAcsiInspection rebuilds draft_data from the items array so an already-filed inspection can be loaded back into the form without data loss.

code.caption: AcsiItem component — pass/fail/NA buttons, sub-field support, and fail-state discrepancy slot
code.note: Clicking the active button again calls onSetResponse with null, clearing the answer. When hasSubFields is true, each A/B/C sub-field gets its own ResponseButtons row and its own discrepancy panel slot.

code.caption: AcsiRiskCert — risk management certification block with editable reviewer blocks
code.note: CERT_TEXT is hardcoded as the regulatory certification statement. Reviewer blocks accept role, organization, rank, name, and title — all rendered read-only in the filed view.

fits: The ACSI module stores its checklist in acsi_inspections.items (JSONB array). The acsi-draft.ts library converts the in-memory responses map → the structured AcsiItem[] format for the DB. Photos attach via the shared photos table using acsi_inspection_id and acsi_item_id FK columns. Activity logging happens only on explicit file/reopen operations, not on every auto-save.

quiz.q: When a new ACSI is started (no ?resume param), when does the DB row get created?
choices: When the user clicks Save Draft for the first time
Immediately on mount, before any items are answered, so photo uploads have a valid acsi_inspection_id
When the user clicks Complete & File
After the first item is answered
quiz.explain: autoSaveToDb is called during the init effect when no resumeId is present — it inserts the row right away so that the photo upload path always has a valid acsi_inspection_id to attach to.

quiz.q: What does `reopenAcsiInspection` do to `draft_data` before updating the row?
choices: It sets draft_data to null so the form starts fresh
It rebuilds draft_data from the filed items array, copying responses and discrepancies back into the JSONB structure
It copies draft_data from the most recent auto-save backup
It fetches draft_data from localStorage on the client
quiz.explain: The function iterates the existing items array to reconstruct the responses and discrepancies maps, then writes that rebuilt draft back to draft_data so the form receives a fully populated state when the user resumes editing.

quiz.q: Which permission key gates the ability to delete an ACSI inspection regardless of whether it has been filed?
choices: ACSI_WRITE
BASE_SETUP_WRITE
ACSI_DELETE
DISCREPANCY_DELETE
quiz.explain: showDelete on the list page evaluates `isAdmin || (canEdit && !isFiled) || (isFiler && !isFiled)`, where isAdmin = has(PERM.ACSI_DELETE). Only ACSI_DELETE holders can delete a filed inspection.

title: Reports
summary: Covers the five report types, the data-builder + PDF-generator pattern shared across all reports, and the analytics dashboard that aggregates cross-module KPIs.
concept:
The Reports module at /reports provides five operational reports generated entirely client-side using jsPDF + jspdf-autotable. No data is sent to a third-party reporting service; the user's browser fetches from Supabase, builds the report object, and renders the PDF inline.

Available reports

Daily Operations Summary/reports/daily — all activity for a selected date or range (inspections, checks, discrepancies, QRCs, outages, obstructions, ARFF status log, photos)
Discrepancy Report/reports/discrepancies — filtered export by status/type/shop/location
Discrepancy Trends/reports/trends — opened vs closed charted over 30d/90d/6m/1y buckets
Aging Discrepancies/reports/aging — tiered by age (0–30d, 31–90d, 91–180d, 180d+) with shop filter
Airfield Lighting Report/reports/lighting — system health, outages, and feature inventory

Data-builder pattern

Every report separates concerns into two files:

  • lib/reports/*-data.ts — fetches raw rows from Supabase with typed interfaces; fan-out queries run in Promise.all() for parallelism.
  • lib/reports/*-pdf.ts — accepts the typed data object, calls createPdf() / drawBaseHeader() / autoTable() from lib/pdf-utils.ts, and returns { doc, filename }.

The page component manages view state ('picker' | 'loading' | 'preview'), triggers the data fetch, then calls the PDF generator on demand. This means the preview can render HTML while the PDF is generated lazily on export.

Daily report date handling

Daily ops uses local date boundaries (the user selects a calendar date, which converts to YYYY-MM-DDT00:00:00 / T23:59:59.999 in the browser's time zone) and converts to UTC ISO strings before the Supabase query. This matches how Airfield Managers think about a shift day.

Analytics dashboard

The /reports index page itself calls fetchAnalyticsData() from lib/reports/analytics-data.ts, which fans out ~10 parallel Supabase queries to populate cross-module KPIs: inspection metrics, check counts, discrepancy open/close rates, QRC response times, wildlife sightings, obstruction evaluations, parking plans, and customer feedback. Results are shown as summary tiles above the report navigation links.

Trends bucketing

The trends data builder uses a client-side bucketing algorithm: it fetches all opened and closed discrepancies in the period in two separate queries, then distributes them into pre-built week or month buckets using timestamp comparison. This avoids a complex Postgres GROUP BY and lets the label format be fully controlled in TypeScript.

code.caption: Daily report data builder — parallel fan-out fetch returning a typed DailyReportData object
code.to:
return {
    inspections: results[0],
    checks,
    newDiscrepancies,
    statusUpdates: results[3],
    obstructionEvals,
    activityEntries,
    qrcExecutions,
    outageEvents,
    arffStatusLog,
    photos,
  }
code.note: Nine independent queries run in Promise.all() so the total latency equals the slowest single query, not the sum of all. Photos are fetched separately after the entity IDs are known.

code.caption: Discrepancy trends — client-side bucket builder and opened/closed placement
code.note: buildBuckets() produces the time-series skeleton; two separate passes then iterate opened and closed rows to place each into the matching bucket. net = opened - closed per bucket.

code.caption: Daily Ops page — three-state view flow (picker → loading → preview) with PDF/email export
code.note: Date picker collects a local date string, converts to UTC ISO for the query, then dispatches fetchDailyReportData + getInspectorName in parallel before transitioning to preview state.

fits: Reports are purely read-only consumers of existing module tables. They carry no RLS write side and never modify data. The pdf-utils.ts helper functions (createPdf, drawBaseHeader, tableStyles, drawFooter) are shared across all 20 PDF generators in the codebase, making visual styling consistent across every export.

quiz.q: Why does the Daily Ops report use local-time date boundaries rather than UTC?
choices: Because Supabase stores timestamps in local time
Because Airfield Managers think of a shift day by their local calendar date, so T00:00:00 in browser time is the correct start boundary
Because the PDF generator cannot handle UTC timestamps
Because all other reports also use local time
quiz.explain: The code comment explicitly states 'Use local time for display — daily ops report is based on local day boundaries'. The user selects a calendar date and the page constructs the ISO string without a Z suffix so the Date constructor uses local time.

quiz.q: What is the purpose of the `buildBuckets()` helper in `discrepancy-trends-data.ts`?
choices: It executes a GROUP BY query against Supabase to aggregate counts server-side
It pre-builds an array of empty time-series buckets that rows will later be distributed into client-side
It converts UTC timestamps to the base's local timezone
It builds the PDF table structure for the trends report
quiz.explain: buildBuckets() creates the skeleton array of TrendBucket objects with opened=0 and closed=0. Two subsequent loops then iterate the fetched rows and increment the matching bucket's counter.

quiz.q: Every report PDF generator must return what shape?
choices: A Uint8Array of raw PDF bytes
{ doc: jsPDF, filename: string }
A base64-encoded string
{ url: string, expires: string }
quiz.explain: CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md both note: 'PDF generators always return { doc, filename }'. The sendPdfViaEmail() helper converts to base64 from doc, and the save path calls doc.save(filename).

title: Activity / Events Log
summary: Covers the append-only activity_log table, the logActivity/logManualEntry write API, cursor-paginated fetch with server-side search, AMENDED detection against daily reviews, and the AF Form 3616-style PDF export.
concept:
The Events Log (/activity) is Glidepath's AF Form 3616 equivalent — an append-only chronological record of every significant operation at the installation. It covers both system-generated entries (created by module CRUD functions calling logActivity()) and operator-typed manual entries (free text or template-based via logManualEntry()).

The activity_log table

Every row carries: user_id, action (verb), entity_type (module noun), entity_id (must be a UUID — for airfield_status rows use installationId, never a string literal), entity_display_id (human-readable), metadata (JSONB — includes the pre-formatted details string), base_id, and created_at. The write functions insert directly into activity_log via the Supabase client; no RPC wrapper is used.

entity_id UUID invariant

entity_id is typed as a UUID in the schema. Passing a string literal (like 'airfield_status') causes an RLS violation. Always use the installation's UUID or the entity's database row ID.

Offline queue timestamp preservation

logActivity accepts an optional createdAt parameter. When an entry is replayed from the offline write queue after the device comes back online, the original timestamp is passed so the log shows when the event actually happened, not when the queue drained.

Two log surfaces

/activityEvents Log — excludes high-volume internal-workflow rows (PPR coordination, wildlife sightings) via excludeEntityTypes. Supports infinite-scroll cursor pagination with server-side search. Shows AMENDED pill when an entry's created_at postdates the day's fully_certified_at.
/recent-activityFull activity stream including all entity types. Used for audit trail and troubleshooting.

AMENDED detection

Each date group in the Events Log page resolves the daily review's fully_certified_at timestamp. Any entry whose created_at is later than that timestamp receives an AMENDED pill — indicating the log was modified after the Airfield Manager signed off the shift.

Server-side search

fetchActivityLogPage() in lib/supabase/activity-queries.ts supports a searchQuery parameter. When the query is ≥2 characters, it first resolves matching user IDs from profiles (name / operating initials), then passes those IDs as an user_id.in.() filter alongside the entity_display_id and metadata.details ilike filters. This means search hits the entire table, not just the current page.

Events Log PDF

The PDF mirrors the AF Form 3616 column order: Date, Time (Z), Action, Details, OI (operating initials), User. It is landscape-oriented and uses the shared pdf-utils.ts helpers for the base header and stat box.

code.caption: logActivity and logManualEntry — the write API for all activity log entries
code.note: logManualEntry sets entity_type='manual' and generates a fresh crypto.randomUUID() for entity_id so the UUID invariant is satisfied even for free-text entries.

code.caption: fetchActivityLogPage — cursor-paginated fetch with server-side search expansion
code.note: PostgREST .or() is single-table only. Matching profiles by name/OI is resolved first as a pre-query, then the matched user IDs are folded into the main activity_log filter.

code.caption: Events Log PDF generator — AF Form 3616-style landscape autoTable
code.note: Returns { doc, filename } like all other PDF generators. Column 4 (OI) is center-aligned and fixed-width 14mm to match the operating-initials column convention.

fits: The Events Log is the audit backbone of the platform. Module CRUD functions call logActivity() after successful writes. The log's entity_id→entity deep-link map in the /activity page UI (getEntityLink()) routes users directly to the linked discrepancy, check, or inspection. The daily reviews system cross-references fully_certified_at timestamps to flag AMENDED entries — a T-3 waiver covers the CAC-signature requirement for Para 2.5.2.10.3/10.4.

quiz.q: What happens if you pass the string literal 'airfield_status' as entity_id to logActivity()?
choices: The row is inserted normally with a string primary key
An RLS violation is thrown because entity_id must be a UUID
The insert silently succeeds but the entry is invisible in the Events Log
The activity-queries fallback path is triggered
quiz.explain: MEMORY.md explicitly states: 'entity_id MUST be a UUID — use installationId for airfield_status rows, never string literals.' The schema enforces the UUID type and RLS relies on it.

quiz.q: An entry is created after the Airfield Manager certifies the shift in daily reviews. How does the Events Log surface this?
choices: The entry is blocked and cannot be added after certification
The entry appears with an AMENDED pill because its created_at postdates the day's fully_certified_at
The entry triggers a new certification requirement for the Airfield Manager
The entry is routed to /recent-activity only and hidden from /activity
quiz.explain: certifiedAtByDate is built from recentReviews in the /activity page. Any entry whose created_at > the day's fully_certified_at receives the AMENDED badge — indicating the log changed after sign-off.

quiz.q: Why does logActivity accept an optional createdAt parameter?
choices: To allow backdating entries for historical data import
To preserve the original event timestamp when the entry is replayed from the offline write queue after connectivity is restored
To support the custom timestamp editor in the Events Log UI
To align entries with the base's local timezone
quiz.explain: The code comment says: 'an AFLD3 off airfield at 14:32Z entry that drains an hour later would carry the drain timestamp and be misleading on the events log.' The original timestamp is passed so the log is historically accurate.

title: Users & Analytics
summary: Covers the invite/approve/role-management flow (service-role admin APIs), page-view telemetry via the record_page_view RPC, and the engagement analytics dashboard at /users/analytics.
concept:
The Users module (/users) provides Airfield Manager and base admin level user management: invite, approve, role assignment, profile editing, password reset, and deletion. All mutations go through Next.js route handlers that use the Supabase service-role client — RLS alone cannot elevate a user's own role.

Admin API architecture

Three route handlers cover the lifecycle:

  • POST /api/admin/invite — creates an auth.users row with email_confirm: true and a known temp password, then updates the auto-created profile to status='active' and must_change_password=true. Sends an email via Resend with the temp password in the body — no deep links to glidepathops.com because Defender for Office 365 quarantines those on .mil tenants.
  • PATCH /api/admin/users/[id] — updates profile fields. Base admins are restricted to their own installation and cannot assign sys_admin or base_admin roles; only system administrators can elevate to those roles.
  • DELETE /api/admin/users/[id] — hard-deletes the auth row. All profiles(id) FK columns are ON DELETE SET NULL (migration 2026061504) so Postgres automatically nullifies actor columns across all 21 affected tables.

Role authorization in API handlers

Every handler authenticates the caller via the SSR client, fetches their profile from the admin client, and checks isAdmin() / isSysAdmin() / canBaseAdminManageUser() helpers from lib/admin/role-checks.ts. The sanitizeBaseAdminUpdate() helper strips sensitive fields (like role escalation) from base admin payloads.

Page-view telemetry

The <PageViewTracker> component is mounted once in the authenticated app shell. On each navigation it debounces 1200ms, checks tab visibility, dedupes the same route within 3s (to avoid React Strict Mode double-effect double-counting), then calls recordPageView(route, installationId).

recordPageView calls the record_page_view SECURITY DEFINER RPC (migration 2026061800). That function stamps auth.uid() server-side and upserts a daily rollup row in page_view_daily: one row per (user_id, base_id, route, view_date) with an incrementing count. Routes are normalized before storage via normalizeRoute() in lib/page-view-route.ts.

Analytics dashboard

/users/analytics loads loadEngagementSummary() from lib/admin/engagement.ts, which aggregates DAU/WAU/MAU counts, module adoption bars, most-visited pages, app version adoption, stale accounts (no activity in 30 days), and per-installation action counts. System administrators see all installations; base admins are scoped to their own.

DAUActive last 24 hours
WAUActive last 7 days
MAUActive last 30 days

code.caption: Admin invite handler — service-role user creation, profile activation, and .mil-safe email
code.note: The route uses getAdminClient() (service-role) for the auth.users insert, but authenticates the caller via the SSR client with the anon key. No deep link is sent in the invite email — only the temp password — because Defender for Office 365 quarantines external links on .mil tenants.

code.caption: User PATCH handler — role guard, base-scoping, and name sync
code.note: sanitizeBaseAdminUpdate() strips fields a base admin should not touch. Only isSysAdmin() callers can assign sys_admin or base_admin roles. Changing first_name or last_name also syncs the legacy name column.

code.caption: Page-view read/write helpers — recordPageView RPC and per-user/app-wide read queries
code.note: recordPageView fires-and-forgets — it never throws. Reads are RLS-gated to admins only. getAppWidePageViews accepts an optional baseId to scope sys-admin queries to a single installation.

code.caption: PageViewTracker — debounced, visibility-gated, deduped navigation tracker
code.note: The 1200ms debounce prevents quickly-passed-through routes from being counted. The 3s dedup window absorbs React Strict Mode's double-effect. No polling — one write per settled navigation.

fits: User management sits at the boundary of Supabase Auth and the application RLS model. The service-role admin client bypasses RLS by design — that is the only way to create users and modify their roles. The page_view_daily telemetry table has RLS on (no policies for unauthenticated readers) so anonymous users cannot see usage data. The record_page_view RPC is granted to authenticated only, never anon.

quiz.q: Why does the invite route NOT include a deep link (e.g. https://glidepathops.com/setup-account) in the invitation email?
choices: Deep links are not supported by the Resend email provider
Defender for Office 365 on .mil tenants quarantines emails containing external https:// links until the domain is on the Safe Links allowlist
The setup-account page is not publicly accessible
Deep links conflict with the must_change_password flow
quiz.explain: MEMORY.md's mil email deliverability note says: 'Defender for Office 365 quarantines emails with https://glidepathops.com/... links until the domain is on the recipient's Safe Links allowlist.' The invite email sends only the temp password; the user navigates to the login page themselves.

quiz.q: What prevents the PageViewTracker from double-counting the same route in React Strict Mode?
choices: A server-side dedup check in the record_page_view RPC
A 3-second client-side dedup window keyed by installationId|route that absorbs the double useEffect call
React Strict Mode is disabled in production so the double effect does not occur
The 1200ms debounce absorbs the double call because both effects fire within the same timer window
quiz.explain: lastRef.current tracks the last key + timestamp. If the same key fires again within 3000ms it returns early. The 1200ms debounce protects against fast route transitions; the 3s window specifically addresses Strict Mode's double-mount pattern.

quiz.q: A base admin tries to PATCH a user to assign them the sys_admin role. What happens?
choices: The update succeeds because base admins have full profile edit access
The route returns 403 Forbidden because only system administrators can assign sys_admin or base_admin roles
The update is silently ignored by sanitizeBaseAdminUpdate()
The route returns 401 Unauthorized because base admins cannot call the PATCH endpoint
quiz.explain: The PATCH handler explicitly checks: `if (updates.role && ['sys_admin', 'base_admin'].includes(updates.role) && !isSysAdmin(callerProfile.role)) { return 403 }`. This runs after sanitizeBaseAdminUpdate() so even if the role field survived stripping, the explicit guard catches it.

title: Base Setup Wizard
summary: Covers the 15-step configuration wizard, the ICAO airport lookup, enabled_modules persistence, and the setup_progress JSONB that tracks per-step completion.
concept:
The Base Setup Wizard at /base-config/setup is a 15-step (~6,000 line) guided configuration page that Airfield Managers and system administrators use to bring a new installation to operational state. Steps are airport-type-aware: some steps (e.g., AEP Agencies) appear only for civilian Part 139 airports; others (e.g., Runway Class) appear only for military airfields.

Wizard steps overview

The WIZARD_STEPS array defines 16 entries (steps 1–15 + the optional AEP step for civilians). The visible subset is filtered at render time using isWizardStepEnabled(step.key, enabledModules, airportType). Required steps show an AlertTriangle if touched but incomplete; optional steps show a Ban icon.

Steps 1–11Required: Runways, Areas, Taxiways, NAVAIDs, CE Shops, ARFF, Facilities, Inspection Templates, Shift Checklist, QRCs, SCN Agencies
Steps 12–15Optional: Wildlife Species, Lighting Systems, Status Boards, PPR Columns, Customer Feedback

ICAO lookup

Step 1 (Runways) offers an Import from ICAO button that calls GET /api/airport-lookup?icao=KVOK. The route handler fetches OurAirports open-data CSV and the FAA NFDC supplement, parses runway geometry, approach lighting, NAVAIDs, and frequencies, then returns a structured AirportLookupResult. This pre-populates all runway rows with FAA survey-grade coordinates, heading, lighting, and surface data — removing the need for manual entry.

enabled_modules persistence

updateEnabledModules(keys: ModuleKey[]) in lib/installation-context.tsx writes the array to bases.enabled_modules and updates the React context optimistically. New default-enabled modules require a backfill migration for existing bases that have a non-null enabled_modules column — otherwise the new module key will not appear in their array.

setup_progress JSONB

markSetupStep(step, status) merges a new entry into bases.setup_progress JSONB: { [stepKey]: { status, completed_at, completed_by } }. The StepperRail component reads this via isStepDone() to show the green checkmark. The context's local state is updated optimistically after the DB write so the UI responds instantly.

StepperRail

The <StepperRail> component renders a responsive grid of step buttons. Status is computed per-step from three sources: the setupProgress JSONB (database), the touched Set (in-memory, reset on page reload), and whether the step is the current one. Mobile: the rail scrolls horizontally and snaps the active step into view via scrollIntoView().

code.caption: WIZARD_STEPS array — step key, number, label, description, and required flag
code.to:
{ key: 'feedback', number: 16, label: 'Customer Feedback', description: 'Configure a public feedback form and generate a QR code. Anyone who scans the code can submit feedback that is stored in your installation\'s database.', required: false },
]
code.note: Step keys are WizardStepKey values from lib/modules-config.ts. isWizardStepEnabled() filters this array before render so the index space of visibleSteps always starts at 0 regardless of airport_type.

code.caption: markSetupStep and updateEnabledModules — the two key persistence functions
code.note: Both functions write to bases via the authenticated Supabase client, then call setCurrentInstallation optimistically so the UI reflects the change without a refetch.

code.caption: Airport lookup route — OurAirports + FAA NFDC CSV fetch and parsing
code.from:
/**
 * Airport data lookup via OurAirports open data + FAA NFDC supplement.
code.note: Two external CSV data sources are fetched: OurAirports for worldwide runway geometry, and FAA NFDC for US-only approach lighting details. The route is rate-limited but not auth-gated — it must work during onboarding before the base is fully configured.

code.caption: StepperRail — status computation and responsive step grid
code.note: statusOf() derives one of five visual states (complete/current/pending/optional/pending_required) from three inputs: setupProgress JSONB, touched Set, and currentIndex. The scrollIntoView call on currentIndex change handles mobile horizontal scroll.

fits: The setup wizard writes only to the bases table (enabled_modules, setup_progress) and to child config tables (runways, areas, taxiways, lighting_systems, etc.). All writes go through the authenticated Supabase client with BASE_SETUP_WRITE permission gating. The Quick Setup flow (QuickSetupModal) offers a fast-path that pre-populates multiple steps from a single ICAO import, drafting into localStorage before the user confirms.

quiz.q: A new module is added to Glidepath with defaultEnabled: true. Existing bases that already have a non-null enabled_modules column do NOT see it. Why?
choices: The enabled_modules column has a NOT NULL constraint that prevents the default from applying
A non-null enabled_modules array is treated as an explicit list; the new key must be backfilled into existing rows via a migration
The module must be enabled manually for each base in the wizard
Only new bases created after the module launch get the default
quiz.explain: MEMORY.md's project_enabled_modules_backfill note says: 'a new defaultEnabled module is invisible on existing bases (non-null enabled_modules only); ship a backfill migration per module.' AMTR was fixed with migration 2026062000; SCN is still missing on 26 bases.

quiz.q: What does isWizardStepEnabled() determine about the WIZARD_STEPS array at render time?
choices: Whether each step has been completed according to setupProgress
Whether each step should be visible based on enabledModules and airportType (e.g., hiding AEP Agencies on military airfields)
Whether the current user has BASE_SETUP_WRITE permission for each step
Whether each step's data has been saved to the database
quiz.explain: The page code: `const visibleSteps = WIZARD_STEPS.filter(s => isWizardStepEnabled(s.key, enabledModules, airportType))`. This is a display filter — it controls which steps appear in the rail, not whether they are complete.

quiz.q: Why does markSetupStep call setCurrentInstallation optimistically after the Supabase write?
choices: To avoid a second Supabase round-trip to re-fetch the bases row after every step save
Because the Supabase write is async and may fail before the optimistic update resolves
To update the StepperRail's currentIndex state
Because RLS blocks reading setup_progress back via the anon client
quiz.explain: The context merges the new step status into currentInstallation locally so the StepperRail's isStepDone() check reflects the completion immediately. Without this, the green checkmark would only appear after a full page reload or refreshCurrentInstallation() call.

title: Customer Feedback
summary: Covers the public QR-scan feedback form (SECURITY DEFINER RPC for config fetch), the FeedbackFormConfig schema, the staff dashboard with inline responses, and the PDF/email export path.
concept:
The Customer Feedback module lets an installation collect visitor feedback via a QR code. The public form (app/feedback/[baseId]/page.tsx) is an unauthenticated route; the staff dashboard (app/(app)/feedback/page.tsx) is behind the permission matrix.

Public form — config fetch via RPC

The bases table has authenticated-only RLS, so a QR-scan visitor cannot SELECT it directly. Instead, fetchPublicFeedbackConfig(baseId) calls the get_public_feedback_config SECURITY DEFINER RPC, which returns only what the public form needs: the base name, a module_enabled flag, and the sanitized form config. If the installation has disabled the feedback module, the form shows a closed message rather than an active form.

FeedbackFormConfig schema

Config is stored as JSONB in bases.feedback_form_config. It controls which optional fields appear (show_name, show_email, show_organization, show_overall_rating), the custom fields array of typed question objects, and the thank_you_message shown after submission. Supported field types: text, textarea, rating, yes_no, dropdown. Responses to custom fields are stored in a responses: Record<string, unknown> JSONB column on customer_feedback.

Submission path

submitFeedback() inserts directly into customer_feedback via the anon client. A client-side rate limit (5-minute cooldown stored in localStorage) prevents accidental double-submission from the same browser session. The server does not enforce a separate rate limit on this path — it is expected to be low-volume.

Staff dashboard

The staff view (app/(app)/feedback/page.tsx) requires FEEDBACK_VIEW permission. It shows a statistics panel (total, average rating, rating histogram, recent count) alongside the raw feedback list. Feedback rows display the comment and all custom-field responses inline — there is no detail screen or click-through modal. The FEEDBACK_DELETE permission gates per-row deletion.

No detail screen by design

Custom-field responses are rendered inline because the form config (and therefore the field labels) can change over time. A separate detail screen would need to reconstruct the label map for each historical entry; the inline approach reads the current fieldLabelMap built from the active config, which is the correct display for the Airfield Manager reviewing responses.

PDF export

The PDF is generated on demand by the dynamically-imported lib/feedback-pdf.ts generator. The staff view offers both Save PDF and Email PDF paths; the email path uses the shared sendPdfViaEmail() helper and the <EmailPdfModal> component.

code.caption: Full feedback data layer — FeedbackFormConfig type, submitFeedback, fetchPublicFeedbackConfig RPC wrapper, and fetchFeedbackStats
code.note: fetchPublicFeedbackConfig merges the returned config with DEFAULT_FEEDBACK_CONFIG so the form always has safe defaults even if bases.feedback_form_config has partial data. If module_enabled is false, the caller receives null and shows a closed message.

code.caption: Public feedback form — RPC config load, dynamic field rendering, client-side rate limit, and submission
code.note: The 5-minute cooldown is keyed by baseId so a device scanning multiple installation QR codes is not globally blocked. The rate check runs before the config fetch so fast-returning visitors see the cooldown message immediately.

code.caption: Staff feedback dashboard — FEEDBACK_VIEW gate, stats panel, inline responses, PDF/email export
code.note: canView is checked in a useEffect that redirects to '/' if denied — so the component never renders confidential submission data to unauthorized users even if they navigate directly.

fits: The feedback module is the only Glidepath module where the write path uses the anon client directly against a table (not a SECURITY DEFINER RPC). The customer_feedback table's INSERT policy allows any authenticated or anonymous user to insert for any base_id — the module-enabled check is enforced by the form UI, not RLS. The config is stored in bases.feedback_form_config and updated via the authenticated client in saveFeedbackConfig(), which requires BASE_SETUP_WRITE indirectly through the setup wizard step.

quiz.q: Why does the public feedback form call get_public_feedback_config() (a SECURITY DEFINER RPC) instead of querying the bases table directly with the anon key?
choices: The anon key has rate limits that the RPC bypasses
The bases table has authenticated-only RLS, so an unauthenticated visitor cannot SELECT from it directly
The RPC applies additional filtering to hide sensitive base configuration fields
RPCs are faster than direct table queries for public endpoints
quiz.explain: The code comment explicitly states: 'bases has authenticated-only RLS, so the QR-scan visitor can\'t SELECT it directly. get_public_feedback_config() returns just what the public form needs.' SECURITY DEFINER RPCs run with the definer's privileges, bypassing the caller's RLS context.

quiz.q: Why are custom-field responses displayed inline on the staff dashboard rather than in a separate detail screen?
choices: The customer_feedback table stores only one response per submission, so there is nothing to expand
The feedback module is read-only and does not support navigation
The field labels come from the current form config, which can change over time; a detail screen would need to reconstruct historical labels per entry
The FEEDBACK_VIEW permission does not grant access to individual response records
quiz.explain: MEMORY.md's feedback note says: 'no detail screen — feedback rows show comment + custom-field responses inline.' The fieldLabelMap is built from the active config, making inline display the correct approach for rendering historical responses with their current labels.

quiz.q: What prevents a visitor who scans the QR code from submitting feedback multiple times in quick succession?
choices: A server-side rate limit enforced by the /api route handler
RLS on the customer_feedback table limits one row per user per day
A client-side 5-minute cooldown stored in localStorage keyed by baseId
The get_public_feedback_config RPC returns module_enabled=false after the first submission
quiz.explain: The form checks `localStorage.getItem('feedback_cooldown_' + baseId)` on load. If the stored timestamp is within 5 minutes, setRateLimited(true) is set and the form shows a wait message. This is browser-local only — there is no server-side enforcement on the submitFeedback() path.
